narrative:
Cindergrove Advisors is a five-person operations consultancy founded in
1995, serving regional manufacturers and distributors. It is a firm of
its era: proposals and memos are typed in Word and printed for the file,
engagement letters come back countersigned by fax and are scanned into
the share by the office administrator, steering-committee decks are
plain PowerPoint with bullet slides, and the annual numbers live in an
Excel workbook kept by the President. Writing is formal but unfussy
mid-90s American business prose: complete sentences, courteous closings,
no buzzwords that would postdate the document. Consultants refer to
clients by company name and to each other by full name in anything a
client might read.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0004",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Plant Consolidation Support for Wagner-Greene

authors.name: Carlos Griffith
authors.title: President
authors.persona: Carlos Griffith runs the firm the way he advises clients to run their plants: on discipline, plain numbers, and a paper trail. He drafts in longhand before anything is typed, keeps the annual workbook close, and reads every proposal that leaves the office. His prose is courteous and unadorned, heavy on complete sentences and light on adjectives, and he signs off with a formal closing even in short memos. He addresses clients by company name and expects the same restraint from everyone who writes under the firm's letterhead.

participants.name: Kimberly Price
participants.title: Senior Consultant
participants.persona: Kimberly Price is the firm's steadiest hand on client engagements, the consultant sent when a manufacturer's operations problem is tangled and the client is anxious. She writes in a measured, methodical voice, laying out findings before recommendations and never promising more than the fieldwork supports. Her steering-committee decks are spare bullet slides with no ornament, and her memos close politely but get to the point in the first paragraph.

participants.name: Andrea Diaz
participants.title: Consultant
participants.persona: Andrea Diaz does much of the on-site legwork, walking distribution floors with a clipboard and turning her notes into clean, workmanlike drafts for review. Her writing is earnest and a touch formal, as befits someone still building her standing with clients, and she is careful to refer to colleagues by full name in anything a client might see. She asks good questions in memos and flags what she does not yet know rather than papering over it.

docs.engagement_summary: Plant Consolidation Support for Wagner-Greene, running about 4 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

facts.hint: amount of money; place where the figure belongs
calendar date; place where the date belongs
proper name; place where the name belongs

docs.guidance: Formal letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement: scope, approach, team, fee and start date, closing. End with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact. 3-6 paragraphs.
